user_prompt:
I have a quick question that is not blackrim related do not save this: 
Longer version:
 
Findings of Canary Deployment Issue
During our cookie-based canary attempt on console.devnew.armada.ai, we ran into a structural conflict with how Istio handles routing on a shared host. Sharing the takeaways since they're relevant beyond just our team.
 
What we observed
console.devnew.armada.ai has roughly a dozen VirtualServices bound to it — one for the frontend (global-mgmt-console) and the rest for backend services (entitlement-svc, organization-svc, asset-svc, etc.). Istio merges all of these into a single virtual host routing table.
The canary cookie-match rule we added on the frontend VirtualService had no uri restriction. After merge, that rule was effectively "match any path with this cookie" and ended up evaluated before the path-specific routes from the other backend VirtualServices.
Practical effect: backend API requests from users carrying the canary cookie were intercepted by the cookie rule and routed to the frontend service instead of the appropriate backend. The frontend's serve -s returned index.html as a SPA fallback — a 200 OK with HTML body. The browser-side queries received HTML where they expected JSON, the data silently returned undefined, and the sidebar fell back to rendering only the tabs that don't gate on entitlement/permission data. No console errors, no failed network requests, because nothing actually failed at the routing layer — the destination was just wrong.
 
What it would take to make canary work properly
Two architectural paths:
 
1- Subset-based canary. Restructure monolith and Turborepo into a single Kubernetes service in one namespace, with two Deployments distinguished by a version label, and use Istio DestinationRule subsets for traffic splitting. This is the textbook Istio canary pattern and resolves the path-conflict problem because subset selection happens within a single destination rather than competing across merged VirtualServices.
The cost is significant: namespace migration for the Turborepo app, Helm chart changes for both apps, CI/CD pipeline updates, and Istio config restructuring across devnew/stage/prod. Several weeks of cross-team work. And it's a temporary structure — we'd unwind it once the monolith is decommissioned, since the only reason to maintain "two versions of one service" is the migration window itself.
 
2-Subdomain isolation (blue-green). Deploy Turborepo on a separate host (e.g., console-next.armada.ai). Its VirtualService binds only to that host, so it doesn't get merged with the backend VirtualServices on the original host. Cutover is a DNS swap at Cloudflare from the old host to the new one. Atomic, reversible by swapping DNS back. This is essentially what prod1 already is.
 
Recommendation
Drop the canary effort and proceed with blue-green via prod1. Our migration is "monolith → Turborepo, then decommission monolith," which is the blue-green shape, not the canary shape. Istio's ca
[...truncated for length...]
